sentences.text: It was the Hlinka Guard.
They came-- they came in--
Slovaks.
Slovaks.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Under whose orders?
Under the Hlinka Guard top man, which wanted me to be his girlfriend.
And I said, you are my-- our neighbor.
You have five children.
And why you need me for a girlfriend?
But I like you.
So I said, I'm sorry.
I don't understand those things.
A father of five children to pick me for a girlfriend.
So he paid me back.
I-- I was take to the city hall.
And there was gathered all the young girls.
And when the young girls wasn't-- they was in hiding, they took their fathers there.

But because I was affiliated with the state school, so I-- I knew if anything happens, I will get a exemption, so I wasn't worried, which I really-- my father went there to the inspector [INAUDIBLE] they call it.
And they issued such a exemption paper that I had to stay.
But I tell you what.
People are very terrible.
There was an [INAUDIBLE].
It is painful that another Jewish girl, who was a religious instruction teacher, she went and bought my papers.
She paid 20,000 crowns for the man who was there in the office and give me the-- the papers of [INAUDIBLE].
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So--
Because my father went to check the-- with the paper.
He said, the papers are not here.
I say-- he said, where are they?
So she said, if you give me 20,000, then I take away the paper from her and give it to you.
But he didn't have 20,000 crowns.
So he was crying.
He came to the city hall, because they wasn't allowed to come in, people.
So-- to the window and he was crying like a child.
And he said to a friend, they tore the-- the nicest flower out from my garden.
That's how he-- he put it.
But I said, don't worry.
I will be all right.
I will.
You could speak to him at that stage.
Yeah.
Yeah.
But for the time being, we-- they took us with the train. 'Shh-shh-shh-shh' train, yes?
Not a train that you go to the city, where you go from one town--
That's--
Yeah.
And they took us with the normal train.
Just adding to show my personality, so I had beautiful clothes.
First of all, I was a kindergarten teacher.
Second of all, the dressmaker, for a long time she lived at our place, because a single girl shouldn't live in a strange town.
It wasn't done.
So-- and I was her model, so I had the nicest dresses.
And I was the only daughter, so my mother and I also.
So-- and now I said, if we are going to the butter factory, we are going to show them that we are working, but we dress up and so.
So I took all my clothes.
And the girls who came from the villages, they was dressed like village girls.
I don't know if you know, but they have one skirt over the other, about five skirts.
And then here just a shirt and like a-- a little vest.
Peasant girls.
So I thought, I have here the long dresses.
Why should they look like-- so I started to divide all my dresses.
I went to the toilet, leave this-- the things here and put on.
Try one, which one you like, it's yours.
How many girls were there with you?
It-- they wasn't with me.
They came to the transport.
So it was about 12 girls I dressed up, so many dresses I had.
What I had, I stayed.
I left-- the-- how you say?
Where you put the-- where you put the things in?
The suitcase.
I left the suitcase.
I went there.
And with us came the Czech gendarme, not-- they wasn't Hlinka Guard.
He couldn't-- he couldn't get over-- he never met somebody who will give away all the dresses, just because to dress up her countryman.
And he said that he will try to take me out, because his uncle is in the ministry.
And when we arrived to the place, it was named Poprad.
Then it-- it didn't look nice already.
Poprad, P-O-P-R-A-D.
Poprad.
Then it didn't look very good.
What did it look like?
Describe it.
It-- it was, you know, a-- it was mish-mash there, you know?
It wasn't like somebody who is continuing to the-- to the butter factory.
It was nobody-- nobody knew.
Nobody was directing.
It was a chaos.
So he said, if you stay here another day, I am sending a telegram to my uncle.
He should send me a telegram back, that you are exempted.
To my bad luck, the same day we went.
And there was waiting the cattle truck and they put us in.
So then I knew.
So you-- you were taken into a cattle truck.
How many people in the truck with you?
To the fullest.
Beg pardon?
Oh, it was no room just to sit and no facilities for toilet facilities, just one little bucket.
And people were suffocated there.
And so much-- if they had some food they ate.
Who didn't bring food, so I shared, because my mother baked and cooked, so I should have that, so I shared.

And then, I knew that in one place which we are passing, not far from Poprad is a place where the Jewish boys are like laboring, not soldiers anymore the-- so I asked the girls to lift me up, because there wasn't a window in that, just-- just a small-- and that was also wired.
But they lifted me up and I just saw there somebody from my town.
And I screamed in Hebrew, they're taking us in the truck, in the cattle trucks.
We don't know where.
Please inquire, find out, and be careful.
After the war, he told me he remembered my message and he tried to give the message further on.
I can't-- I can't remember really exactly, because all the time while I was there, I occupied myself to quieten those people down and to attend to this one.
You know, I-- I [LAUGHS] took over the-- the first aid like.
I still said, if we will keep together and we will help each other, it won't be so bad, I said.
But then when we arrived to Auschwitz--
They was very stressed and there was crying.
And it was very uncomfortable, because they couldn't lie down and they didn't have anything to drink, anything to eat.
And the facilities, to toilet facilities, it was very, very depressed.
Beg pardon?
Terrible.
So many people together and no proper facilities.
Terrible.
Yeah.
Must have been more, about two days.
About two days.
I don't know.
Really, I don't know why I forgot this part, how long it took.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
No.
No, no.
No.
They didn't die.
No.
Just they were-- because most of them there was young woman.
This was a-- a transport with young, unmarried woman.
So with them, I-- you know, on the same level.
I said, don't worry and don't panic and don't-- you will see.
We will hold together and-- and we will be all right.
OK.
So you get to the end of the journey.
Where-- where do you come out?
We arrived there.
We didn't know what it-- where it is.
The SS mans open the door and the dogs were barking.
And he said, [NON-ENGLISH].
It was lucky for me.
I didn't have any luggage, so I just had what I was, the coat, the bag, and a muff.
So I jump down, because I was very sport-minded.
But some girls had their luggage, you know, and they thought--
So I-- so they got stranded.
They beating because they couldn't jump down.
It was quite high and especially when you had luggage in your hand.
So this was-- you said you had a muff.
It was cold?
Yeah.
It was winter.
Winter in '42.
And this place, I said it, the devil found that place, Auschwitz-Birkenau.
Beg pardon?
The SS, the SS man.
Yeah.
I said-- I said to the girls, let's run so they haven't got a reason and they can't reach us.
But-- when you have to carry and-- and so you can't run.
And then they put us in a barrack.
They-- they-- they they accompanied us to the barrack.
And we went there, dehydrated, tired.
And the German woman who came a few days earlier-- they brought up some drink, but they said it's poisoned.
And we've been so dehydrated and so thirsty, and that-- that tea, it wasn't real tea.
It was a brew made from some leaves.
But still, it was warm and it was liquid.
So I said, listen, girls.
We have to drink.
Otherwise we won't last.
I am going to drink it.
If I am not back in half an hour, then don't drink it.
If I come back, how bad it is, you hold your nose and drink it.
That was in Auschwitz.
And in Auschwitz, they had toilets.
No.
The kettles was standing before the barracks.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I don't know if I had a-- a dish, what kind of dish I had.
Can't-- that I can't remember.
But I must have had a dish.
I was drinking like you said, with the nose, holding.
And and it wasn't poisoned, so I said, girls, we have to drink.
And then they brought some food in.
Again they said it's poisoned.
So again, I said, I'm going to try it.
It was rotten-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some-- I don't know how you call it.
But what they found, you know, the-- the peasants put that for the winter under the earth.
You know, it was a custom that you put over the winter some vegetables or even potatoes under and they cover it with lot of earth.
And then they uncover it and it's still edible.
But before-- but because the peasants, they wasn't there, the-- the Germans made them run-- go further on and this place was only for them.
So the food was under the earth.
When they opened that, so it was half-rotten.
So that we-- from that, they made the soup.
So I went and I said, I'm going to try it.
Now, it was really terrible.
Because I was in the toilet, so here I drank and here I vomited.
But I did that a few time, 'til it settled down.
And I came.
I said, girls, we have to eat it, because you are hungry, dehydrated.
You have to get used to it.
So that was our first welcome.
And then there was nothing there in that room.
So the girls went--
There was 600.
But it's a huge room.
And so they wanted to undress the coat and put it nicely together.
So I said, lie in the coat, because it's more-- you can't-- you will be cold and uncomfortable.
Have a look what I will do.
I put the muff under my head and I lie in my coat, which had fur collar and fur [INAUDIBLE] and I put my hands in so I shouldn't be so cold.
I [INAUDIBLE] I saw that they are very naive and not practical.
They taking off the coat.
I said, if here we lie, you will have coat.
And if not, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that's it.
Next day, they [NON-ENGLISH], that means 'run, run, run,' beat us and-- so I said, girls, run so you wouldn't be hit.
Yeah.
And then they let us stand.
And lot of girls collapse, fainted.
For hours in the cold.
This is-- they-- it wasn't the camp for that, for surviving.
It was just for suffering and for killing.
So they let us stand there.
A lot of girls fainted.
And there was another girl from city, Bratislava.
She was also a sport girl, so we two had made ourself the-- that they will carry the girls somewhere inside, in a barrack, so they should recover.
Nobody was there.
They-- we were standing, that's what I said.
Standing for hours.
Nobody said anything.
We didn't know what will happen.
No.
After a few hours, when we've been already so tired and-- so they took us to the washroom.
We-- they-- we had to undress quickly and naked.
And then they put us in big bins, 10 in a very dirty something water.
And then naked we came out.
Men came and took all the hair from everywhere, naked as we've been.
They was laughing.
Anyway, and then when we've been half-frozen, so they gave us a remnants of Russian prisoner of war things what left over, which was like a shirt-type and pants.
Often the-- the shirt and pants didn't have any button or just one button or so.
So sometimes girls had to hold onto it.
And then--
Beg pardon?
No.
That-- they took it away.
And then they selected and they send that to Germany.
Beg pardon?
That kind of a-- it was-- it was a green-colored cotton shirt.
Mine didn't have a-- a button here.
And the pants, I didn't have a button here.
So I hold on to it.
And later on, we started to dig in the bed or in the mattress, which was very low, and digged in.
And I for good luck found two pieces of string, so I straight away had at least something so I didn't have to hold it so strong together.
So the first day, we didn't do anything.
They just left us there.
And--
In the barracks.
But then it came, the counting.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In the morning, they woke us up about 3 o'clock, [NON-ENGLISH] for the counting, because the German woman, there was very primitive prostitutes and thieves and murderers.
So they didn't know the counting to do.
And the same was the SS woman.
It was [? the ?] thousand German woman for the start and a thousand SS woman.
And they somehow couldn't count us together.
It was not only our barrack.
It was more coming.
So we had to stand for hours 'til they counted us.
Cold.
And that first day, they put us back, because they didn't have the program for us.
And then the next day, they was looking for people to work inside, in the kitchen, in the [INAUDIBLE], in the so-called hospital, in the-- where they sew, sew room.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so that was, because they had to establish-- we started a new camp.
That's why they-- they had to establish it.
But I didn't go nowhere, because I was occupied with four girls who lost their mind for a while.
And so I was trying to bring them back to the consciousness that they are not crazy, just a shock what-- what happened.
They couldn't believe that it's possible.
Beg pardon?
No.
I did remember one, but I've forgotten, so it would take me long to bring her back to memory.
But I was very surprised at how good they are now after few days.

And so they carried me around and they said, we want Magda, we want Magda, because they were so grateful, because they knew if somebody will found out that they are crazy, then the-- straight away the news went on that they got a phenol injection to the heart and they would be dead in a minute.
So now when they found out that I saved their lives, so they were very grateful.
And then it happened that the woman in our room-- which was over 600.
It was a big room.
There was a German, a woman but she was [NON-ENGLISH].
She wasn't from those prostitutes or murderer.
Not a--
--Jewish German woman.
She lived in Warsaw.
Yeah.
Her husband was a highest medical researcher and doctor.
And she was his wife.
No.
I was so much with her together, but I can't remember her name.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she-- she was the one who asked me, why don't you look for-- for a job, like inside job?
So I said, I want to go out for agriculture.
I want to prepare myself for Palestine, I said.
When the war will over-- be over, then we will be able to go to Palestine.
So I want to go and prepare myself how-- how to work in-- in agriculture.
So she told herself I must be crazy or very naive.
So she asked, what are you from profession?
So I said, what I am from profession, I don't want ever to be-- to have it here.
But still, tell me what it is?
I'm a kindergarten teacher.
So she said, I am a teacher.
So now it became like a bond.
And the next day, when the-- the woman all went to their position what they have to work, so I went to the agriculture colony.
So she came and she gave me two [? slaps ?].
And she said, you are coming with me.
I need your help.
You will be doing cleaning, fetching the food, fetching the-- the tea and the kettles and the bread and everything.
So-- and I said, where will I clean the floor and clean the floor?
You said you will scratch the dirt with your nails.
Anyway, I did that-- I told myself, oh, I am not so silly.
I'm going to agriculture.
And that's why I tried a few time.
She slapped me a few times.
And then she had enough, so she said, what you think that these are agriculture?
How long do you think you will last there?
They don't want to teach you agriculture?
They are just here for killing.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Yeah?
So she said, you will be working inside with me and help me, because I can't-- you see my legs are swollen.
I can't work so much.
So that's-- so I did.
I went for tea.
I always speak some girls.
I explained to them, we have to go.
And I can't carry for 600 people, so who is volunteering?
Some did.
And if they didn't, so I said, you have to come.
So how did you have the power to tell them to come with you?
Just told them.
I-- I became-- from this woman, she was the [NON-ENGLISH] and I became a [NON-ENGLISH] helper.
